title: Self-Declared benchmarks and fund manager intent: “Cheating” or competing?
abstract: Using a panel of self-declared benchmarks, we examine funds’ use of mismatched benchmarks over time. Mismatching is high at the beginning of our sample (45 % of TNA in 2008), consistent with prior studies, but declines significantly over time (27 % in 2020), driven by existing specialized funds changing benchmarks to match their style. Market forces including investor learning, institutional governance, market competition, and product positioning all play a role in benchmark correction decisions. For funds with difficult to categorize investment strategies, mismatched benchmarks are less associated with performance bias. Our study highlights the value of market solutions in aligning manager-investor interests.

author.Huaizhi Chen: Mendoza College of Business, University of Notre Dame, Notre Dame, IN 46556, USA

article.1. Introduction: Relative performance evaluation (RPE) is a theoretically-motivated solution to moral hazard that is commonplace in modern incentive contracts.1 Both firm executives and fund managers typically have bonus compensation based on performance relative to a group of peer firms, peer funds, or other comparable benchmarks.2 At the same time, a growing body of empirical evidence indicates that the incentivization tools based on RPE are limited in their efficacy by executive or managerial influence. The incentivized parties often have sway over the selection of the comparable sets. In the case of CEOs, while compensation committees often hold the responsibility for defining the peer group, the choice of these peers is likely to be influenced by CEOs (Bizjak et al., 2008), especially when relative power of the CEO is high (see, also, Dikolli, Diser, Hofmann, and Pfeiffer, 2018). In the case of mutual fund managers, Sensoy (2009) finds that 40.4 % of U.S. equity funds select incorrect benchmarks which overstate their relative performance, resulting in higher flows. Similarly, Cremers, Fulkerson, and Riley (2022) document that 24 % of funds have a high degree of benchmark mismatch. These results reveal how strategic distortions of the comparison group or benchmark limit the practical effectiveness of RPE incentivization.In this paper, we explore both the nature of these distortions and the economic forces that alleviate them by analyzing the evolution of fund benchmarks. In contrast to the 2004 cross-section used in Sensoy (2009), our dataset consists of multiple snapshots of U.S. equity fund benchmarks collected between 2008 and 2020. Using the “mismatched benchmark” Morningstar definition from Sensoy (2009), we find consistent results that at the beginning of our sample, 34.2 % of funds (45.4 % of fund assets) have mismatched benchmarks. However, over our sample period, the percentages of mismatched funds (assets) decreased steadily to 27.2 % (27.8 %). Moreover, the fraction of fund assets benchmarked to the S&P 500 decreased from 52.4 % to 38.7 %. The decrease in mismatched benchmarks is largely driven by the intensive margin (i.e., changes in benchmarks for existing funds), not the extensive margin (i.e., opening of matched funds and closing of mismatched funds). Almost 60 % of funds with mismatched benchmarks in 2008 that survive to 2020 switched to a matched benchmark over the course of our sample. Formally, a linear model of the trend in mismatched benchmarks demonstrates that the equal-weighted likelihood of mismatch decreases by 0.762 % per year within our sample period (2008 to 2020).Correctly interpreting the frequency and the trend in mismatching requires accounting for the breadth of the fund's investment strategy. While Holmström (1982) assumes the presence of a perfectly matched and sufficiently diversified RPE peer comparison set, such a peer group may not exist.3 Because benchmarks are often defined within an investment category (e.g., Russell Mid Cap Growth), funds whose strategy extends beyond a single category may have difficulty identifying a “matched” benchmark. In contrast to the prior CEO and mutual fund literature which suggests that such mismatching is consistent with strategic distortions (or put more plainly, “cheating”) on the part of the CEO/fund manager (e.g., the fund exhibits a positive bias relative to the benchmark, see Garvey and Milbourn (2006)), this alternative explanation suggests that the selection of such benchmarks is related to risk, not performance.To capture funds whose investment approach extends beyond an easily categorized style, we calculate the degree to which a fund manager invests outside her core investment objectives. This new measure, “Investment Strategy Breadth”, or IS Breadth, is the percentage of a given fund's holdings outside its Morningstar assigned investment objective category. We further demonstrate that IS Breadth exhibits a positive correlation with other approximations for portfolio breadth and flexibility.The IS Breadth measure contributes to explaining both the trends and the levels of mismatched benchmarks. First, when we allow the linear trends to depend on the levels of IS Breadth, the rate of decline in mismatched benchmarks is 1.06 % per year for funds with IS Breadth one standard deviation (SD) below the mean (the more specialized funds), and 0.482 % for those with IS Breadth one SD above the mean (funds with a broader investment strategy). This trend differential suggests the potential for economic forces leading to more accurate benchmarks for specialized funds, while broad funds are less likely to make “corrections” due to the unavailability of appropriate benchmarks.Second, we find that specialized funds with mismatched benchmarks exhibit higher return bias than broad funds do: Their chosen benchmark is easier to beat, consistent with performance gaming. In contrast, the broad funds’ Mismatches appear attributable to hedging motives or a lack of suitable benchmarks for broader strategies. We further find that at fund inception, funds with more specialized portfolios tend to select benchmarks with lower market and SMB factor exposures than the matched benchmark the fund later switches to, again in line with a performance gaming interpretation.We examine potential economic forces that may explain the correction of benchmarks among the specialized funds. Within the principal-agent setting, we examine investor learning and improved institutional investor oversight that result in more sophisticated principals. We then go beyond this agency setting and consider product market competition, including product differentiation and the desirability of product features. Within these channel analyses, we separately account for IS Breadth, reiterating the critical role of strategy breadth in the effectiveness of RPE and funds’ responses to the economic factors.Investor learning is the idea that as investors become more financially sophisticated, they are better able to assess both the correct benchmark for the fund and the potential efforts of the manager to obfuscate. How do investors learn? One possibility is that in competitive markets, rival firms will educate consumers about potential obfuscation to win over educated customers (Shapiro, 1995).4 The market for investor education has been evolving. First, passive advisors such as Blackrock, State Street, and Vanguard, who would benefit from debiasing, have invested heavily in investor education, including benchmarking and performance measurement.5 Second, third-party information providers such as Morningstar and Lipper often provide a fund performance comparison to that of the average of the portfolio category, which approximates for a “matched” benchmark. Third, the rise of fiduciary financial advisors can steer investors towards more transparent products. Together, these educational efforts may result in more sophisticated investors, driving the shift in actively managed firms’ behavior away from mismatched and towards correctly matched benchmarks, because efforts to obfuscate are less effective. Yet, in the case of broadly invested funds, whose initial mismatched benchmarks may not be chosen for obfuscation, these efforts will likely not affect the incidence of mismatch.In examining investor flows before and after the change of benchmarks, we show that for specialized funds, not only do the average investors assess manager performance relative to the correct benchmark even before the switch, but that they appear to only respond to the new benchmark if it is appropriate to the fund's investment style. Though this level of investor sophistication may be surprising for the literature, it is consistent with the intensive investor education initiatives described above. For broad funds, in contrast, while we find evidence of investors responding both to the new and old benchmarks, we find no change in flow sensitivity even after a switch to a matched benchmark. In response to such investor learning, specialized funds are more likely to switch to matched benchmarks when the difference in investor sensitivity between the correct benchmark and the self-declared benchmark is large.The second channel through which principal monitoring may improve is the growth in institutional investor oversight. Prior work shows that institutional investors can help mitigate moral hazard, benefiting retail investors in other fund products overseen by the same fund managers (e.g., Evans and Fahlenbrach (2012)). Thus, the existence of an institutional version of the same fund allows for governance by the institutional investors who are more capable of both assessing the suitability of a benchmark and influencing the choice of a correct benchmark. Comparing funds with and without institutional twins, we find that having an institutional twin lowers the probability of selecting a mismatched benchmark for specialized funds.After examining the investor learning and monitoring channels, we turn to an important factor in competitive equilibria – product differentiation.6 Considering benchmarks as conveying information on the differentiation of fund objectives, we find that as competition in a given benchmark category increases (as measured by the number and market share of index fund competitors), funds, especially those with specialized portfolios, are more likely to switch to benchmarks that match their portfolio styles.7In addition to the direct measures of price competition, we also examine the product characteristics upon which the prior literature has shown investors base their investment decisions. Sensoy (2009) has shown, and we confirm in our sample, that investor flows are sensitive to the performance of the fund relative to its benchmark. Additionally, the prior literature has shown that more sophisticated investors are sensitive to fund relative risk/tracking error (e.g., James and Karceski (2006), Del Guercio and Tkac (2008)). Separately, from an RPE perspective, a lower tracking error can help hedge risk for the risk-averse manager (Gibbons and Murphy (1990)).8 We find that funds are more likely to switch to a mismatched benchmark when relative performance improves, and more likely to make a correction when tracking errors are reduced. In a related paper by Mullaly and Rossi (2022), the authors find evidence consistent with funds changing their benchmarks to look better in relative performance terms. In contrast, we show that the economic channels of risk positioning, product differentiation, as well as the learning and governance, contribute to the equilibrium of benchmark choice in the equity fund market.In addition to exploring the application and effectiveness of RPE, our study contributes to two strands of literature. First, the paper complements existing studies which highlight intermediaries as information providers that guide retail investor decisions, and particularly those demonstrating the improvement in the quality of such information provision. On this topic, Blume (1998) underscores the importance of selecting appropriate peer groups for evaluating mutual fund performance, and Evans and Sun (2021) show a major refinement in the peer groups used by Morningstar in 2002 has led to more sophisticated investor risk adjustment. Our study, in contrast, concentrates on the enhanced quality of self-declared benchmarks of mutual funds which has occurred gradually over time. The similarity is that both the rating change and the benchmark switches we document in this paper are partially prompted by the demand of (relatively sophisticated) investors.Second, while performance benchmarks can result in more informed fund choices by retail investors and better alignment of fund manager incentives, the broader literature shows that they can further influence the asset demand by (incentivized) fund managers and ultimately asset prices. Recent studies, for example by Buffa and Hodor (2022); Pavlova and Sikorskaya (2023); Kashyap, Kovrijnykh, Li, and Pavlova (2023); and Kim (2024) document the distortions caused by inelastic and correlated demand that originates from benchmarking practices. Further, Han, Roussanov, and Ruan (2022) shows that the risk-taking incentives by fund managers who lag their benchmarks lead to risk anomalies. Thus, if the trend we document in this paper continues, distortions caused by the largest benchmarks, such as the S&P 500, may continue to decline, but demand associated with more differentiated benchmarks may create new return anomalies.The remainder of the paper proceeds as follows. Section 2 describes the data sample. Section 3 introduces the IS Breadth measure. Sections 4 through 7 comprise the results. Section 8 concludes.
article.2. Data: 2.1. Databases and sample constructionMotivated by a desire to enhance investors’ ability to evaluate fund performance, the SEC in 1993 began requiring mutual funds to select a “broad-based securities market index” and report graphically the performance of both the fund and its benchmark to enable investor comparisons.9 These performance comparisons are reported in each fund's annual prospectus and annual reports, and the choice of a performance benchmark can change from report to report.In order to capture the evolution of self-declared benchmarks over time, our analysis uses snapshots of the Morningstar database collected between December 2008 and December 2020. These snapshots contain the listings of self-designated prospectus benchmarks of equity mutual funds operating in the United States and the contemporaneous index characteristics. We match these snapshots to the fiscal reporting dates of our mutual fund sample to obtain the dates on which the benchmark changes become effective.10 Because we use the Morningstar style box classification to categorize both individual funds and the various benchmark indices, we restrict the sample to Morningstar-designated U.S. equity funds. The combined equity fund times month panel- with their benchmarks- therefore captures the aggregate, as well as individual, evolution of equity fund benchmarks over time.All in all, we have 3,709 unique actively managed equity funds using 154 individual benchmarks making 806 benchmark changes across our sample. This data is supplemented with additional information on monthly characteristics including historic fund returns, Morningstar Ratings and style boxes from Morningstar Direct. Additional information on individual fund holdings at quarter ends was licensed from Morningstar. Table 1 reports basic summary statistics of our sample. Definition of mismatched benchmarksFollowing the method in Sensoy (2009), we consider an equity fund to be mismatched if its self-designated prospectus benchmark has a different equity style than the fund's Morningstar Category. Specifically, during our sample period, Morningstar Categories correspond to the 3-by-3 holdings-implied style-boxes that describe a fund or a benchmark's size focus (Large Cap, Medium Cap, and Small Cap) and investment styles (Growth, Blend, and Value). If a fund had designated the S&P 500 - a Large Cap Blend index- as its prospectus benchmark but was categorized as a Large Cap Value fund by its holdings, we code this case as a Benchmark Mismatch. This classification may be sensitive to the boundaries of style boxes set by Morningstar; therefore, we also investigate the robustness of our findings using continuous measures of benchmark mismatch (see Appendix B).Panel B of Table 2 describes equity funds by the styles of both their holdings and their benchmarks. Each column represents a different holding-implied equity style, while each row represents a benchmark style. The top of this panel divides up the sample using size target, while the bottom does this using growth-value orientation. Funds with Large Cap stock holdings use the most size-style appropriate benchmarks with over 99 % of the fund-month observations having a benchmark that indicated a Large/Broad size target. This consistency deteriorates in the Small Cap and Mid Cap categories- with 4 % of the Small Cap and 25 % of the Mid Cap funds using some type of a Large/Broad Cap benchmark. In the growth-value dimension, there is a greater likelihood of mismatch between funds and their benchmarks. The most severe disagreement occurs in the Growth and Value fund categories: 41 % of Growth and 32 % of Value fund-time observations use a Blend benchmark.Panel C of Table 2 reports summaries of the benchmark switches over our sample. There are 806 changes in the reported primary benchmarks. Contributing to the industry wide trend, 38 % of these changes occur when a fund switches from a mismatched to a matched benchmark. In contrast, there are 136 benchmark changes (17 % of all the changes) that move an equity fund from using a correct to a mismatched benchmark in our sample. This represents the least common type of switches, and we offer more discussion of such changes in Section 4.2. We also report in Panel C that only a small number of these incidents of benchmark switches are accompanied by a “style drift” – measured as a contemporaneous change in the Morningstar style box.2.2.2. Primary vs. secondary benchmarksMutual funds sometimes display their returns in comparison to more than one benchmark index. Morningstar reports a “primary prospectus benchmark” for all funds and a “secondary prospectus benchmark” when applicable. This designation of “primary” and “secondary” is made by the investment advisor and communicated to Morningstar.Our analysis focuses on the primary benchmarks for four reasons. First, while the fund may include a secondary benchmark in its prospectus, the primary benchmark is more visible to investors. For the majority of our sample period, Morningstar only included the primary prospectus benchmark in its default fund performance information.11 Similarly, in examining media mentions, third-party reporting and the marketing materials of several funds, we found that often the relative performance display only included the primary benchmark index (see Appendix C for several examples). Second, because the primary benchmark is self-designated by the fund to Morningstar, it is likely the more important benchmark (usually the benchmark disclosed right after fund performance and before other benchmarks), thus it can be more important for the fund's own marketing purpose and is more likely to influence investor behavior. Third, there is likely to be a mechanical negative relationship between the matched/mismatched designation of primary and secondary benchmarks. Funds conceivably choose multiple benchmarks to provide different metrics of their risk exposure. As a result, there is little rationale for choosing two highly correlated benchmarks (e.g., S&P 500 and Russell 1000). Instead, when the primary benchmark becomes more matched over time, the secondary benchmark may mechanically become more mismatched over time. Fourth, using only the primary benchmark creates a more robust measure of mismatching. For funds with multiple benchmarks, the incidence of mismatching can be maximized (minimized) if a fund is considered to be mismatched when only one benchmark (both benchmarks) is (are) mismatched. Instead, we focus on the economically most relevant benchmark.
article.3. Investment strategy breadth (IS Bread: In the principal-agent setting proposed by Holmström (1982), a given agent is judged relative to a set of appropriate peers, such that as the number of peers increases, firm-specific risk is diversified away and the peer set then perfectly reflects and (via RPE) hedges the common risk faced by each agent. In practice, finding a sufficiently large set of appropriate peers may not be so simple. For mutual funds, this issue may be magnified in that many benchmarks are designed to reflect the equities and risk within a given investment objective. For funds whose strategy extends beyond a single category, identifying an appropriate benchmark that reflects the common risk experienced by the manager is more difficult. Because this key difference in investment strategy may affect the economics underlying the choice of a mismatched benchmark, we propose, validate, and then utilize a novel measure of the breadth of an investment strategy: IS Breadth.3.1. IS Breadth measure constructionIn the simplest terms, investment strategy breadth is the degree to which a fund manager invests beyond her core investment objective. We rely on Morningstar's split of the US equity mutual fund universe (and equity universe as well) into nine categories based on the market capitalization (i.e., small-, mid-, and large-cap) and relative valuation (e.g., book-to-market) of their underlying holdings.12 We use the Morningstar's designation of each fund's core investment objective category and then sum the percentage of the fund's holdings that fall in the other eight style boxes to create a raw measure. To facilitate the interpretation of the coefficients on the variable later in the analysis, we standardize IS Breadth to have a mean of zero and a standard deviation of one across all observations.133.2. IS Breadth validation of conceptAs an important point of validation for our novel measure, in Table 3, we report the relationship between the standardized IS Breadth and several other measures of portfolio breadth. Panel A reports the means of these alternative portfolio breadth measures, and Panel B shows OLS regression coefficients on IS Breadth for the other breadth measures. All regressions include Morningstar-box (of the portfolio)-by-time fixed effects, and the standard errors are clustered by fund.Table 3. We assume that funds committed to having specialized portfolios along the market cap and/or growth-value dimensions are likely to include those keywords in their names. Out of our sample, a third of funds do not indicate a specialization in their names (which we call Name Broad, Panel A). Panel B column (1) shows that a one standard deviation higher IS Breadth is associated with 8.4 % higher probability (or 25 % of the mean) that the fund has a broad name. Column (2) adds fund fixed effects which controls for the highly persistent fund name and IS Breadth, and the R-squared rises above 90 %. Even with these fixed effects, there is a statistically significant coefficient showing that a fund with a one SD increase in IS Breadth has a 0.7 % increase in the probability of switching to a broad name.Funds with broad strategies may also migrate among the Morningstar style classifications. The second measure we examine (columns (3) and (4)) is an indicator that equals one if a fund experiences a style drift, as defined by a change in its Morningstar style box designation in the following 12-month period. The Morningstar style boxes are defined by the centroids of the fund holdings, and we expect the centroid to move around more for funds with broad portfolios. The unconditional average likelihood of style drift is a rather low 4.7 % in our data. However, columns (3), (4) suggest that a one SD higher IS Breadth (either in the cross section or in the time series within fund) is associated with a 5–6 % higher likelihood of a style drift. The coefficients are large and suggest that the style drifts observed in the data may be entirely driven by the funds with broad holdings.Finally, we turn to the funds’ investment descriptions in their prospectus and create two approximations for the breadth of the investment mandate. We classify a fund's investment mandate as broad if the description simultaneously contains opposing equity styles, namely, “large” and “small”, or “growth” and “value”, at the same time. Alternatively, if the description indicates specific percentages, it is likely that the fund has a mandated allocation to certain styles, implying less breadth.14 28 % and 76 % of the mandates are broad and specific, respectively, according to our approximated measures. Columns (5), (6) of Table 3, Panel B, show that IS Breadth is positively correlated with broad mandates and negatively correlated with specific mandates.Overall, the evidence in Table 3 validates our construction of the IS Breadth measure. This measure appears to capture meaningful variation in the degree to which certain funds cannot be easily categorized in the simple 3 by 3 (Large, Medium, and Small by Value, Blend, and Growth) setting.
article.4. Benchmarking trends: 4.1. Trends towards fewer mismatched benchmarks4.1.1. Graphical evidenceWe begin our analysis by plotting the time series trends in benchmarking accuracy across all equity mutual funds. Fig. 1, Panel A, plots the percentage of assets with mismatched benchmarks making up the sample over time. In December 2008, 34.2 % of actively managed equity funds had mismatched benchmarks. This estimate is similar to the 40.4 % of funds that have substantially different market exposures from their self-designated benchmark measurement in Sensoy (2009) which utilized a single cross-section of active equity funds from 2004. However, our novel panel data allows us to track the systematic changes in benchmarking in this market. Consistent with improvements in relative performance evaluation, we observe a steady decrease in mismatched assets (individual funds) from a high of 45.4 % (34.2 %) of the sample in December 2008 to 27.2 % (27.8 %) by December 2020.15 Panel B shows that funds that change their benchmark over time (i.e., the intensive margin) drive the trend, as the pattern follows closely the result in Panel A. Estimation of linear trendsTable 4 explicitly estimates linear trends in the incidents of mismatched benchmarks, both overall and by levels of IS Breadth. In column (1), we first relate Benchmark Mismatch to a Trend variable while controlling for fund fixed effects. Trend is the total time since the start of the sample, calculated as the number of years and the fractional equivalent of months (each month accounts for as 1/12th of a year). The inclusion of fund fixed effects focuses the estimation on the intensive margin and controls for unobservable (time-invariant) fund characteristics. The estimation indicates that on average, the likelihood of mismatch decreases by 0.762 % per year. Next, in columns (2) and (3) we control for Standardized IS Breadth and further interact it with Trend, to allow differential trends for specialized and broad funds. In column (3), the coefficient on Std IS Breadth implies that at the start of our sample (Year 0), funds with IS Breadth one SD above the mean were 2.37 % less likely to have mismatched benchmarks than funds with the average portfolio breadth. Interpreting the interaction coefficient together with the baseline, the result suggests that the rate of decline in mismatched benchmarks is 1.06 % (0. 771 %+0. 289 %) per year for funds with IS Breadth one SD below the mean (specialized funds), and 0.482 % (0. 771 %−0. 289 %) for those with IS Breadth one SD above the mean (broad funds). Incidents of switching to mismatched benchmarksWhile we analyze all benchmark changes systematically in our later analysis, it is instructive to consider the shift from a style-matched to a mismatched benchmark separately. On the surface, this change seems most egregious in its possible RPE strategic distortion as suggested by Sensoy (2009) and Mullaly and Rossi (2022). However, upon individual examination, we find that many of these changes occur for three simple reasons: 1) switching the benchmark to match the style implied by the fund's name; 2) investment advisor merger resulting in a benchmark change to ensure uniformity across funds; and 3) changes in index licensing agreements of a fund family. This simple examination reveals that for 51 % of these changes, not only is the underlying rationale sensible, but even with these changes, we eventually observe a shift toward benchmark consistency. We elaborate on these findings in Appendix F. Moreover, in considering these changes through the lens of IS Breadth, broad funds account for 69 % of these changes.4.3. Performance evaluation and investor choicePrior literature has shown that the prospectus benchmark determines how investors interpret a fund's past performance (Sensoy 2009). We replicate this result for our sample by estimating the impact of self-declared benchmark adjusted returns on investor flows, after controlling for other measures of fund performance. In Table 5, we measure investor flow in three ways. We use the percentage change in market share ( % Change in Market Share) as proposed by Spiegel and Zhang (2013), an indicator variable of positive flows (Positive Flow), and the percentage fund flows ( % Flow) which is the most common flow measure in the literature. The measure of the 36-month self-declared benchmark adjusted return is Raw – Benchmark Return. Importantly, we control for other main performance metrics, including the 36-month Fama-French Three-Factor Adjusted Alpha (36 Month Alpha), and the average overall Morningstar Ratings (Star Rating).17 All flow measures are forward-looking monthly data in month t + 1, while performance measures are backward-looking (reported using data up to month t). We also include date (year-month) times investment style fixed effects to absorb any variation in investor flows to styles over time. Table 5 shows that even after controlling for the risk-adjusted returns, Morningstar ratings, and other fund characteristics, investor flows respond significantly to benchmark adjusted returns across all regression specifications. Baseline relationship between is Brea: In this section, we dive into several factors that explain the levels of mismatched benchmarks, before moving on to explain the trends in the next section. We hypothesize that higher portfolio breadth is associated with more difficulty in selecting a matched benchmark. As a result, when broad funds select mismatched benchmarks, the motive may be more aligned with hedging, instead of performance manipulation. Conversely, funds with more specialized portfolios have well-defined benchmarks, making their choice of mismatched benchmarks potentially strategic. We show that strategic considerations at fund inception may explain the baseline level of mismatch for these funds. However, as we explain in the next section, strategic gaming appears to be increasingly disciplined by market forces during our sample period.5.1. Bias and variance of mismatched benchmarksTo evaluate whether the incidences of Benchmark Mismatch are consistent with motives to game performance or hedge risk in broader investment strategies, we examine the Bias and Variance associated with the fund's specific benchmark choice. These measures are constructed using the fund's best-fitting benchmark from the set of 12 most popular benchmarks identified by Sensoy (2009). Specifically, Bias is the average difference between the monthly returns of the best-fitting benchmark and the fund's self-declared benchmark (best-fitting minus self-declared) over the past 36 months. Variance is the average squared deviation between the two benchmarks over the past 36 months. A positive Bias is consistent with possible gaming in performance evaluation - the fund has selected a poorly performing benchmark over its best-fitting benchmark. A large Variance shows that the return variation over time of the selected benchmark poorly matches that of the fund. While managers could select benchmarks to minimize either bias or variance (Geman, Bienenstock, and Doursat (1992)), a choice that generates a positive bias without an accompanying reduction in variance is consistent with performance gaming.In Table 6, we examine the sub-sample of funds with mismatched benchmarks and regress Bias and Variance on IS Breadth to explore the above hypotheses. All regressions include Morningstar portfolio style box-by-time fixed effects, as well as benchmark fixed effects (to control for any unconditional bias and variance from each benchmark) and standard errors are clustered by fund. The first specification (Column (1)) shows that high IS Breadth funds tend to exhibit lower Bias in their selection of a primary benchmark index. One standard deviation higher IS Breadth lowers the benchmark Bias of a mismatched fund by 0.0132 % cumulatively over a 36-month period. In the multivariate specification (Column (2)), we observe a similar magnitude of IS Breadth coefficient at 0.0127 %.Table 6. Bias, Variance, and IS Breadth among Mismatched Funds. Specialized funds: evidence for gaming at fund inceptionComplementing the analysis of possible best-fitting benchmarks, in this subsection, we examine the subsample of funds that switched their benchmarks over time. This subsample enables us to study how relative benchmark returns (possibly reflecting gaming motives) influence a fund's initial benchmark choice relative to their future benchmark.18 For instance, if a mismatched fund began with the S&P 500 as its primary benchmark at its inception and eventually switched to, say, Russell 1000 Growth, its initial selection of the S&P 500 – if for gaming motives – will capture a manager's expectation that the S&P 500 would underperform the Russell 1000 Growth benchmark. Alternatively, mismatched funds without gaming motives may have chosen the S&P 500 for other reasons (for example, following the industry norm), which would imply no future return differential between the two benchmarks.In Panel A of Table 7, we examine the systematic risk loadings or betas from a standard FF 3-factor regression of the fund's “initial benchmark” (the one chosen at inception) over the first 36 months of the fund's existence. We compare that to the fund's “final benchmark” (the one the fund switches to in the future), calculated over the same 36 months. The panel reports the results from a regression of the difference in benchmark betas (initial minus final) for each factor on Mismatch, Std. IS Breadth, and the interaction of the two. The negative coefficient on Mismatch for the market (columns (1) and (2)) and size (SMB) (columns (3) and (4)) beta differences indicates that the initial benchmark has lower risk loadings on these two factors than the final benchmark for the initially mismatched funds. The size factor result is consistent with the fund/benchmark holdings comparison shown in Panel B of Table 2, which shows that a sizable percentage of mismatched funds with mid- and small-cap holdings choose a benchmark with larger market cap. For the book-to-market (HML) beta (columns (5) and (6)), the initial benchmark on average has a higher loading than the final benchmark for those mismatched funds. The result that the initial benchmark has a higher value tilt may appear surprising at first. However, our sample period is one where growth funds on average outperform blend and value. In other words, the value premium was negative during our period. Thus, initially selecting a benchmark with a stronger exposure to the value factor may in fact strengthen the perceived relative performance. We further explore this idea below in Panel B. Finally, looking at the Mismatch * Std. IS Breadth interaction term, we see that the difference in SMB and HML for the mismatched is partially reversed for broader funds, suggesting a possible alternate motivation for selecting mismatched benchmarks.Table 7. article.6. Economic channels: The empirical finance literature finds that the effectiveness of RPE in mitigating agency costs is limited by CEO/fund manager's capture of the peer group/benchmark selection process. In the mutual fund setting, our evidence of shifts from mismatched to correct benchmarks suggests that other economic forces may help reinforce the effectiveness of RPE by reducing such capture. To explore the underlying economic forces, in this section, we turn broadly to two sources. First, drawing from the principal-agent framework, we examine possible improved monitoring by more sophisticated principals. Our investigation includes investor learning and institutional investor governance, which are discussed in 6.1 Investor learning, 6.2 Institutional investor monitoring, respectively. Second, we examine the effects of both product market competition and product characteristics in 6.3 Competition, 6.4 Product market characteristics – relative return and risk. Given our evidence that the use of “mismatched” benchmarks by broad funds is consistent with hedging and not with gaming motives, we control for (standardized) IS Breadth and its interactions with the main economic drivers under investigation in these analyses.6.1. Investor learningThe evidence from the prior literature suggests that mismatched benchmarks arise out of agency settings with unsophisticated principals. That is, biasing relative performance by choosing a mismatched benchmark is more likely when investors don't know what constitutes a “matched” benchmark. We therefore hypothesize that the incidence of such cheating may be decreased if investors become more sophisticated monitors through learning.If investors become aware of the nature of the mismatched benchmark disclosures by fund managers, or if they learn about the correct peer group from other sources, that may diminish the influence of a fund's self-designated benchmark. As discussed earlier, this process of “debiasing” may be facilitated by investor education efforts from passive competitors (e.g., Vanguard and BlackRock), third-party entities, or incentive-aligned financial advisers. These sources can inform investors of the true, holding-based benchmark of a fund, regardless of the fund's self-declared benchmark. We call the adjustment in investor behavior, prompted by the recognition of inadequate benchmarking, and the correction of misinformation the investor learning hypothesis.As a first evaluation of the investor learning hypothesis, in Table 8, we return to the flow sensitivity analysis, focusing on how investor flows behave before and after benchmark switches. In each case of a fund switching benchmark indices, we examine the relationship between investor flows and returns adjusted by both the prior benchmark and the new benchmark in the 24 months before and 24 months after the observed change. We also separately examine the impact of Std. IS Breadth by repeating the analysis for specialized funds (Panel A) and broad funds (Panel B), which are defined relative to the median Std. This analysis yields two important insights. First, for percent flows (specifications (9)-(12)), we see that the sensitivity of flows to the new benchmark is not driven entirely by the post-switch period; instead, investors already respond to the new benchmark for return adjustment even before the switch, when the fund was still using the old benchmark (coefficients on Raw– New BM Return without the interaction with Post Switch). Thus, for specialized funds this is consistent with investors paying attention to benchmark switches only when they improve matching. While some sophisticated investors use the new benchmark, predating (and potentially driving) the benchmark switches, our results are consistent with another group of investors evaluating the old and new benchmarks at benchmark switching decisions, and they rely on the new benchmark only when it is matched to the fund's investment style.Moving to Panel B, we see important differences for the broad funds. First, there is stronger investor sensitivity to new benchmark relative to old benchmark adjusted performance across all specifications when compared to the specialized fund results in Panel A. Second, after conditioning on post switch period and benchmark switch type (e.g., mismatched to correct), we find no statistically significant evidence of an increase in investor flow sensitivity to the new benchmark (specifications (2)-(4), (6)-(8), and (10)-(12)), although we find some limited evidence of a reduction in sensitivity to the old benchmark after the change. This is consistent with the lack of a clearly superior benchmark for broad funds and as a result, broad fund investors, in contrast to specialized funds, respond less to the benchmark changes.One possible explanation for why mismatched benchmarks declined over time for the specialized funds is the increased investor sophistication (access to unbiased information) over our sample period. To further explore this hypothesis, we investigate the investor flow sensitivity to past returns separately for equity funds with correct and mismatched benchmarks. If investors have learned to be less reliant on mismatched benchmark returns, we would expect the difference between the sensitivities of these groups increases over time. We find evidence of this hypothesis both in the time series dimension and in the cross section of equity fund flows sensitivities.Consistent with the learning hypothesis, we observe seemingly different investor responses to past return performance for the sample of correct and mismatched equity funds. Institutional investor monitoringBuilding on the investor learning results, an alternative channel to improved monitoring is institutional monitoring. We hypothesize that due to their sophistication, institutional investors see through both the Bias and Variance of funds’ benchmark selections and demand benchmarks that reduce both (if possible) in RPE. In addition, if a retail fund serving unsophisticated investors is simultaneously monitored by sophisticated institutional investors, then such a fund is less likely to have a mismatched benchmark. Again, this hypothesis mainly applies to specialized funds. Two potential barriers may limit the influence of institutional governance on broad funds. First, institutional investors, due to their sophisticated asset allocation strategies, may opt for specialized over broad funds. Second, even when a broad fund is held by institutional investors, the scope to improve the benchmark is limited because of the ambiguous nature of what may constitute a best benchmark for the fund.We start by studying institutional investors’ demand for benchmark quality in Table 10. CompetitionWhile enhanced monitoring is an obvious potential explanation stemming from the RPE literature, product market competition provides a separate economic channel that may positively affect the selection of matched benchmarks. In this section, we explore how direct measures of market competition might affect the decline of mismatched benchmarks. Regardless of whether investors are aware of the correct benchmark of a fund, they see the self-declared benchmark prominently displayed on the fund's webpage, prospectus, annual reports, or advertising, and are likely to interpret it as information on product positioning. For example, a fund benchmarked to the S&P 500 may be viewed as holding the large-cap segment, even if the fund's actual portfolio has a differentiated objective. Thus, funds with the same benchmark or benchmark category may compete with one another regardless of their portfolios.We hypothesize that a fund is more likely to switch its benchmark when the category of its current benchmark is subject to greater competition. This hypothesis is grounded in the industrial organization literature demonstrating that companies can engage in product differentiation to soften price competition (e.g., Shaked and Sutton, 1982). As competition rises among the funds with the same benchmark category, switching to a less crowded category can reduce the competitive pressure, and the switch may also lead to a benchmark correction. Given the discussion in Section 3, this hypothesis applies primarily to funds with specialized portfolios. While specialized funds with a mismatched benchmark have a natural strategic response, namely switching to a correct specialized benchmark, which would also better hedge the systematic risk the manager is exposed to, for broad funds, the switch to a more specialized and therefore differentiated benchmark would subject the fund manager to additional risk. Moreover, broad funds have already differentiated on the basis of greater investment strategy breadth, switching to a specialized benchmark while the fund strategy remains broad is not an obvious strategic choice.In the sector of actively managed funds, one important source of competition comes from index funds which offer benchmark index returns at low fees (Cremers, Ferreira, Matos, Starks 2016; Sun 2021). The economic channel of index fund competition is two-fold. First, penetration of index funds measures the intensity of price competition and the commoditization of a category, motivating product differentiation. Second, to the extent that the transparency surrounding index funds de-biases investors, index fund competition is associated with investor learning which also leads to benchmark corrections.We consider two measures of the competitive pressure from index funds: the number of passive funds in a benchmark category (including index mutual funds and exchange-traded funds, below we call both “index funds” for simplicity) and the market share of index funds in that benchmark category. We construct index competition measures at the benchmark category level, following the Morningstar classifications of benchmarks, so that funds with benchmarks in the same category are considered as competing with one another.23Table 11 presents the estimates of the relationship between competition from index funds and funds’ benchmark switches, estimated with probit models and monthly observations. To capture only benchmark differentiation while holding the portfolios constant, we exclude all funds with changes in Morningstar style classifications from the estimation. Because of the heterogeneity in IS Breadth and the potential liability of having mismatched benchmarks varies across style categories, we include Morningstar style box-by-time fixed effects. Thus the comparison is between funds with the same portfolio classification but benchmarked to index categories that are subject to different levels of competition. We further interact the competition measures with Std. IS Breadth to shed light on whether competition affects specialized funds and broad funds differently. We cluster the standard errors at the benchmark category level because that is the level of variation in the competition measures.Table 11. Product market characteristics – relative return and riskWhile the last section considers direct measures of product market competition, now we examine product characteristics upon which the prior literature has shown investors base their investment decision: performance and risk. In terms of performance, Sensoy (2009) has shown, and we confirm in our sample, that fund investment decisions are sensitive to the performance of the fund relative to its benchmark. In terms of relative risk, James and Karceski (2006) and Del Guercio and Tkac (2008), among others, have shown that more sophisticated investors are sensitive to tracking error. For tracking error specifically, there is also a separate potential motivation that arises from the RPE literature. Gibbons and Murphy (1990) suggests that selecting a more closely matched benchmark (i.e., a lower tracking error benchmark) can help to hedge risk for the agent (manager), inducing greater effort. The anecdotal evidence that investment advisors use tracking error as an overall risk control and link it to manager compensation further reinforces this second motivation for examining tracking error.Why then consider relative return and risk in terms of benchmark switches? Because the reference benchmark is used to estimate these two relative quantities, switching benchmarks could potentially improve one or both measures, increasing demand from investors. While the prior literature highlights higher relative performance as a benefit of declaring mismatched benchmarks, mismatched benchmarks may also increase perceived risk (tracking error). Therefore, we hypothesize that funds trade off higher relative performance with higher tracking errors to decide whether to select mismatched or matched benchmarks.We test this tradeoff hypothesis in Table 12 using monthly data. We calculate the difference in the 36-month benchmark-adjusted performance had a fund switched to the new benchmark from its current self-declared.24 If the new benchmark improves the fund's relative performance, the difference variable (BM AdjRet Diff (New-Old)t) would be positive. For relative risk, we calculate the tracking error of each fund around their current self-declared benchmark as the standard deviation in the monthly benchmark-adjusted returns during a 36-month period. For all funds, we also calculate the counterfactual tracking errors had they switched to the most widely used benchmark in the matched benchmark category and take the difference in the two tracking errors (under the counterfactual/correct benchmark minus under the current benchmark, (TrackErr Diff (Correct-Old)t). The difference should be exactly zero for funds that are currently benchmarked to the most widely used benchmark in their matched category, and close to zero for other funds with matched benchmarks. For the funds with mismatched benchmarks, we expect the difference to be negative, i.e., the funds can reduce their tracking errors (improving this characteristic) by switching to the matched benchmark.Table 12. Managerial entrenchment: Given that corrections in benchmarking may be advantageous from the perspective of investors flows, corporate governance, and hedging the impact of systematic on the fund manager, the question remains: why haven't all specialized funds corrected their mismatched benchmarks? article.8. Conclusion: In 1993, the SEC began requiring mutual funds to select and disclose a “broad-based securities market index” to mitigate concerns about the potential moral hazard of excess risk-taking, thereby recognizing the potential for relative performance evaluation (RPE) to mitigate moral hazard in the mutual fund industry. In that disclosure, the SEC explains that “The choice of an appropriate benchmark is important in evaluating performance because it is important to compare apples to apples.” They also indicate that investors should be wary of funds that select style mismatched benchmarks, suggesting they “should question why that benchmark was used.”While this focus on correct risk-adjustment and the SEC's recognition of the potential for relative performance evaluation (RPE) to mitigate moral hazard is to be commended, there were at least three aspects of the SEC's regulatory approach to this issue that fell short. First, the regulatory guidance uses the S&P 500 as an example of an appropriate benchmark which may have led to the overuse of this often style-mismatched benchmark. Second, the SEC's guidance on what constitutes an “appropriate index” lacks clear direction about how the benchmark's investment style should relate to that of the fund. Third, the SEC directs the fund itself, not an independent third party, to select the appropriate benchmark. Given these three shortcomings, the evidence documented by the prior literature that style-mismatched benchmarks are frequently selected by funds is not surprising and highlights a potential cost with RPE.In this paper, however, we find i) not all mismatching appears to be guided by strategic rent extraction concerns, and ii) forces outside the explicit incentive contract have power to effectively “monitor” the manager, decreasing the incidence of mismatched benchmarks and the associated moral hazard.Understanding the economics of benchmark choices in the RPE framework is important for policy implications. For broad funds, the choice of mismatched benchmarks does not appear to be driven by rent extraction considerations. For specialized funds, however, if principals remain naïve and agents choose benchmarks for the purpose of rent extraction, regulations aimed at restricting benchmark choices could potentially improve consumer welfare. However, we show in this paper that market forces including investor learning and competition work together to mitigate the bias in benchmark selection, and this implies potential value in investor education, competition for sophisticated investors, as well as reducing managerial entrenchment.
